metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: No `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` items found.
findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state that the reconcile.py:586 comment ('route_inbound_probe stays here...') will be removed/updated as part of this ticket, and confirm whether route_inbound_probe is intended to move (re-export) or stay in reconcile.py.
evidence: Plan Seam: 'route_inbound_probe' listed as one of the 10 symbols moved to reconcile_helpers.py
reconcile.py line 586: '# route_inbound_probe stays here because it is a separately-tested public surface.'
test_inbound_probe_dispatch.py loads reconcile.py and calls reconcile.route_inbound_probe directly
findings.finding: The plan's Seam section lists `route_inbound_probe` among the 10 symbols to be MOVED to reconcile_helpers.py, but the existing reconcile.py code comment at line 586 explicitly states 'route_inbound_probe stays here because it is a separately-tested public surface.' The plan does not acknowledge or resolve this contradiction — a reader cannot tell whether (a) the comment is stale and will be updated, or (b) the plan intends something different from what the comment says.
findings.location: Seam section (10 moved symbols list) vs. reconcile.py:586
scenarios: Implementer follows existing code comment and leaves route_inbound_probe in reconcile.py, resulting in only 9 symbols moved and a larger-than-expected reconcile.py.
Implementer moves route_inbound_probe but leaves the stale comment, causing future maintainer confusion about why it is in reconcile_helpers.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Seam section: 'The existing code comment at reconcile.py:586 ("route_inbound_probe stays here...") is stale and will be deleted; route_inbound_probe moves to reconcile_helpers.py and is re-exported, preserving its public API.' If the intent is that it stays in reconcile.py, remove it from the 10-symbol list.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean

verify command `wc -l src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile_helpers.py` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

verify command `wc -l src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the docs/architecture.md deletion in AC3 — e.g., `grep -c 'reconcile_passes' docs/architecture.md` asserting output `0` (or the exact stale row text) so the doc edit is independently verifiable.
evidence: AC3: 'its remedy row deleted from docs/architecture.md'
AC4 (test_module_size_contract.py) exercises gate files only, not docs/architecture.md
No grep or diff command is listed for the docs edit
findings.finding: AC3 states that the remedy row must be deleted from docs/architecture.md, but no proving command is provided to verify this deletion. The pytest gate (AC4) validates gate-file changes but does not read docs/architecture.md, leaving the doc edit unverifiable from the listed commands.
scenarios: An executor completes the gate-file changes and all tests pass, but forgets or mis-edits the docs/architecture.md row; all ACs appear green with no failing check to catch the omission.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: 'Verified by: `grep -rn "reconcile_passes" docs/architecture.md | wc -l` returns 0 (or grep exits non-zero when the row is absent).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `StatusMappingError` in `reconcile_helpers.py` should be eliminated in favour of re-importing from `_errors.py` (which already carries the canonical definition used by `applier.py`), rather than moving the reconcile.py duplicate to a third file.
evidence: `_errors.py` line 126: `class StatusMappingError(Exception): ...` — canonical production definition already exists
`applier.py` line 63 imports `StatusMappingError` from `_errors.py` (via `_errors_module.StatusMappingError` at line 99)
`reconcile.py` line 22 defines a second copy: `class StatusMappingError(Exception): ...`
Plan lists `StatusMappingError` among the 10 symbols to move to `reconcile_helpers.py`, creating a third definition / re-export chain
Test `test_errors.py` line 69 asserts `issubclass(errs.StatusMappingError, Exception)` from `_errors.py` — these two classes are NOT the same object
findings.finding: The plan proposes moving `StatusMappingError` from `reconcile.py` into `reconcile_helpers.py` (and re-exporting it back through `reconcile.py`), but `_errors.py` already defines a canonical `StatusMappingError` at line 126, and `applier.py` already imports it from `_errors.py`. Moving the reconcile.py copy into a third module (`reconcile_helpers.py`) perpetuates a duplicate class definition rather than consolidating onto the existing canonical one, violating the NIH sub-criterion.
findings.location: Seam section — '10 moved symbols' list; reconcile.py line 22; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_errors.py line 126; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py line 63
scenarios: A test that does `reconcile.StatusMappingError is errs.StatusMappingError` (or any isinstance check across the two) will fail because they are distinct class objects loaded from different module copies.
Future code that catches `_errors.StatusMappingError` will silently miss raises from the `reconcile_helpers` copy, creating latent bug surface.
findings.suggested_fix: In `reconcile_helpers.py`, instead of re-defining `StatusMappingError`, import and re-export it from `_errors.py`: `from rebar_reconciler._errors import StatusMappingError` (or via `_load('rebar_reconciler_errors', '_errors.py')`). Then `reconcile.py`'s re-export points to the same class object as `applier.py` does, eliminating the duplicate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Seam section whether `_commit_binding_store_snapshot`'s `from rebar_reconciler import git_adapter` and `_read_local_tickets`'s `from rebar._engine import in_process_cli` must be converted to `_load()`/`lazy_load()` calls in the new leaf (as run_differs.py does for all its sibling deps), or document why the direct package imports are safe for standalone by-path loading.
evidence: reconcile.py line 162: `from rebar_reconciler import git_adapter` — inside _commit_binding_store_snapshot body, a direct package import, not _load()
reconcile.py line 371: `from rebar._engine import in_process_cli` — inside _read_local_tickets body, a direct package import, not _load()
run_differs.py lines 34-58: uses lazy_load/_load for all sibling deps; no direct `from rebar_reconciler import ...` package imports in function bodies

tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_commit_binding_store_failure.py:27 loads reconcile.py by spec_from_file_location — if git_adapter is a direct package import in the new leaf, standalone loading may fail when rebar_reconciler package context is absent
findings.finding: The plan asserts the new leaf module will 'lazy-load ITS OWN siblings (config.py, alert_store.py, mutation.py, inbound_probe.py, git_adapter)' via the lazy_load+_load bootstrap boilerplate, but two of the 10 symbols being moved use direct package imports instead of _load(): `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` imports `from rebar_reconciler import git_adapter` (line 162) and `_read_local_tickets` imports `from rebar._engine import in_process_cli` (line 371). These are NOT _load()-routed. The plan does not specify whether these imports must be converted to _load() calls as part of the move — yet conversion IS required for the new standalone leaf to be loadable by-path in tests without package context (the same constraint run_differs.py handles via _load()).
findings.location: Seam section — 'The leaf reproduces the lazy_load+_load bootstrap boilerplate (identical pattern to the sibling run_differs.py) so it lazy-loads ITS OWN siblings (config.py, alert_store.py, mutation.py, inbound_probe.py, git_adapter)'
scenarios: If git_adapter and in_process_cli imports are left as direct package imports in reconcile_helpers.py, spec_from_file_location loading in the test suite (which does not guarantee package context) will hit ImportError — breaking the 'existing tests are the oracle' guarantee stated in AC5.

Even if tests currently pass reconcile.py by-path (because the package IS importable in the test environment), the plan's stated rationale for using _load() is exactly to avoid this fragility — leaving two package imports unaddressed is inconsistent with the plan's own stated design.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `_helpers = _load('reconcile_helpers', 'reconcile_helpers.py')` must appear AFTER the lazy_load bootstrap block and `_load()` definition (i.e., after ~line 266 in the current file), not at literal module top.
evidence: reconcile.py: lazy_load bootstrap block at ~line 239, _load() defined at line 258
Plan says 'loads the leaf once at module top' without acknowledging the ordering constraint
run_differs.py follows the same pattern: lazy_load bootstrap then _load() function, sibling loading happens inside functions not at module top
findings.finding: The plan states reconcile.py 'loads the leaf once at module top via `_helpers = _load(...)`', but the `_load` helper function in reconcile.py is defined mid-module (after the lazy_load bootstrap block at ~line 239). After the 10 symbols move, the `_helpers = _load('reconcile_helpers', 'reconcile_helpers.py')` call and the 10 name-rebinding statements must appear AFTER the `_load` definition — 'module top' is imprecise and could mislead the implementer into placing the call before `_load` is defined, causing a NameError.
findings.location: Seam section: 'reconcile.py loads the leaf once at module top via `_helpers = _load(...)`'
scenarios: Implementer places `_helpers = _load('reconcile_helpers', ...)` before the lazy_load bootstrap or _load def, causing NameError on module import.
Implementer uses a different loading strategy (e.g., lazy function-level loading) instead of a single module-top call, leading to inconsistent behavior with the described pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'loads the leaf once at module top' with 'loads the leaf immediately after the _load helper is defined (post-lazy_load bootstrap), binding each re-export as a module-level name.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `_helpers = _load('reconcile_helpers', 'reconcile_helpers.py')` call and re-export bindings must be placed AFTER the `_load` helper definition (i.e., after the lazy_load bootstrap block at ~line 258 of the current reconcile.py), not at literal module top.
evidence: reconcile.py lines 243-266: `lazy_load` bootstrap + `_load` definition appear at ~line 258
reconcile.py line 131: `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` (one of the 10 moved symbols) is currently defined BEFORE `_load`

Plan phrase: 'reconcile.py loads the leaf once at module top via `_helpers = _load("reconcile_helpers", "reconcile_helpers.py")`' — 'module top' is ambiguous given `_load` is not available until line ~258

run_differs.py (the pattern reference) places its own `_load` definition at lines 49-58 and never calls `_load` at module top (only within functions) — so the existing pattern reference does not demonstrate a module-top `_load(...)` call
findings.finding: The plan says reconcile.py loads the new leaf 'once at module top via `_helpers = _load(...)`', but `_load` is defined mid-file (~line 258, after the lazy_load bootstrap). Placing the `_helpers = _load(...)` call and re-export bindings before `_load` is defined causes a NameError at import time. The plan does not specify WHERE in reconcile.py's file order the re-export block must appear, leaving an ordering ambiguity that an implementer could get wrong.
findings.location: Seam section — 'reconcile.py loads the leaf once at module top via `_helpers = _load(...)`'
scenarios: Implementer places `_helpers = _load('reconcile_helpers', 'reconcile_helpers.py')` near line 22 (true 'module top'), before the lazy_load bootstrap; Python raises NameError: name '_load' is not defined on reconcile.py import.

Re-export block placed after `_load` definition but before `_PassContext` dataclass, causing the `_NoOpSyncLogger` re-export to shadow any module-level usage that `_load_snapshots` references before the re-export line — unlikely but order-sensitive.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Seam section, replace 'reconcile.py loads the leaf once at module top' with: 'reconcile.py loads the leaf immediately after the `_load` helper definition (the lazy_load bootstrap block) via `_helpers = _load("reconcile_helpers", "reconcile_helpers.py")`, then binds each of the 10 names as a module-level re-export: `preflight_status_mapping = _helpers.preflight_status_mapping`, etc.'
